results.possibleIdentity: Robert Fiala is a Canadian artist, filmmaker, and photographer who was active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Based in Montreal, he was a recipient of bursaries from the Canada Council for the Arts in the category of Cinema and Photography for the 1969-1970 and 1970-1971 fiscal years. Fiala is notably associated with the Multigravitational Group (also known as Aerodance), an experimental performance company founded by Stephanie Evanitsky. He served as the group's Assistant Artistic Director, lighting designer, and company manager in the early 1970s. The Multigravitational Group was known for its innovative 'multigravitational' dance and theater works that utilized a large, 20-foot high scaffolding structure, blending physical performance with complex architectural and technical elements. This work aligned with the goals of organizations like the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which fostered collaborations between artists and engineers; a grant for E.A.T. Montreal and Toronto was notably documented in the same Canada Council report that listed Fiala as a bursary recipient.

results.possibleIdentity: Christopher Tree (often referred to as Chris Tree) is a percussionist, musician, and sound artist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his 'Spontaneous Sound' performances, which were improvisational concerts typically held outdoors using a vast collection of global percussion instruments, including Tibetan temple gongs and flutes. Tree gained significant counterculture recognition for opening each day of the Woodstock Festival in 1969 with a performance of his gongs. He was a figure in the avant-garde and experimental art scenes, associated with the circle of artists involved in the Fluxus movement and Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His work was documented in the 1967 short film 'Christopher Tree' by filmmaker Les Blank. In the late 1960s, his performances were supported by the New York State Council on the Arts, appearing in reports alongside the foundational activities of E.A.T. Archival recordings of his music have been released on the Quakebasket label.

results.possibleIdentity: Salle Werner (now known as Salle Werner Vaughn) is an American surrealist and multi-media artist born in 1939 in Tyler, Texas. Active since the late 1960s, her work encompasses painting, drawing, collage, assemblage, and large-scale immersive installations. She is particularly recognized for "Harmonium," a long-term art installation in Houston consisting of a series of early 20th-century cottages that she transformed into thematic, evocative environments. In 1973, she was included in the Whitney Biennial at the Whitney Museum of American Art under the name Salle Werner. Her work is held in permanent collections including the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and the Museum of Fine Arts, Houston. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was a part of the experimental art and music scene in Texas; she maintained a studio in Dallas used by experimental composer Jerry Hunt and was active in the Houston alternative art scene alongside figures like Robert Whitman, a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Koller is identified as a papermaker and artistic collaborator who, along with her husband John Koller, founded HMP (Handmade Paper) in Woodstock Valley, Connecticut, in the early 1970s. The Kollers became renowned for producing high-quality, specialty handmade paper used by major contemporary artists, including those associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) such as Robert Rauschenberg, Dan Flavin, and Ellsworth Kelly. Their work is characterized by the technical and creative collaboration between the artist and the papermaker, a hallmark of the E.A.T. movement's focus on bridging art, technology, and craft. Works printed on 'HMP Koller' paper are included in major museum collections, such as the National Gallery of Art and the Smith College Museum of Art.

Marilyn Koller was a student in the Art Department at the University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB), graduating in the Class of 1967. During her time at UCSB, she was active in an environment that emphasized contemporary art and large-scale exhibitions, such as 'Five Centuries of Master Prints.' This historical timeframe (late 1960s) and her academic focus on fine arts align with the period of activity and the potential association with the regional art and technology scene in California during the rise of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
